text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY — SURVEILLANCE FOLLOW-UP NOTE

ATTENDING: Head and Neck Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established patient, routine surveillance

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Nine-month post-treatment surveillance visit following definitive chemoradiation for HPV-positive left base of tongue squamous cell carcinoma.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Margaret Chen is a 52-year-old never-smoking woman of East Asian descent with a history of Stage III (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition, HPV-positive staging) squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue (p16 strongly positive, HPV-16 confirmed by ISH), who returns today for her 9-month post-treatment surveillance evaluation.

By way of brief summary: the patient initially presented approximately a year ago with left-sided throat discomfort and a palpable left level IIA cervical lymph node. Staging workup confirmed a 2.2 cm left base of tongue primary with a single left level IIA metastatic node and no distant disease. NGS profiling of the primary tumor revealed a PIK3CA p.E545K hotspot mutation along with co-occurring alterations in TRAF3, KMT2D, EP300, FBXW7, FAT1, and NOTCH1; TP53 was wild-type, consistent with HPV-associated HNSCC. PD-L1 CPS was 18. She was discussed at multidisciplinary tumor board and treated with definitive concurrent IMRT (70 Gy in 35 fractions to the primary and gross nodal disease) and high-dose cisplatin (100 mg/m2 q3 weeks). She completed two of three planned cisplatin cycles; the third was held due to a transient creatinine elevation to 1.32 mg/dL during week 6. Treatment course was complicated by grade 3 confluent mucositis, significant odynophagia, and a 10.5% body weight loss requiring PEG tube placement at approximately week 5 of radiation. IMRT was completed without interruption. Post-treatment PET-CT at 12 weeks confirmed complete metabolic response at the primary site and involved node, corroborated by CT neck at 6 months showing resolution of all previously enlarged lymphadenopathy. PEG tube was successfully decannulated 8 weeks following the 12-week post-treatment assessment.

Today, the patient is approximately 9 months out from completion of chemoradiation. She is doing well overall and reports no new concerning symptoms. Specifically:

- Swallowing: markedly improved. She is eating a near-normal diet. She does note occasional difficulty managing very dry or tough-textured foods (crackers, crusty bread, well-done meats) and continues to keep liquids at hand during meals, but reports no frank aspiration events, no coughing or choking episodes, and no sensation of food sticking. She denies regurgitation. Solid-to-liquid transit she describes as slightly slower than before treatment, which is expected.
- Xerostomia: persistent grade 1, her primary ongoing complaint. She uses artificial saliva spray (Biotene) and takes frequent sips of water throughout the day. She notes it is somewhat worse at night. No new dental complications; she continues with fluoride trays as recommended by her dentist.
- Voice/hoarseness: none. Her voice is intact, which is important to her given her role as a music teacher.
- Pain: no throat pain at rest or with eating. She is not taking any analgesics.
- Constitutional: energy levels improved significantly. She has returned to part-time teaching (three days per week) and reports that she is enjoying the return to work. Appetite good. Weight stable.
- Ear: no otalgia. Mild high-frequency hearing loss (4 and 8 kHz) confirmed on audiogram following treatment; she does not find this functionally significant at this time.
- Neurologic: mild tingling sensation in the fingertips persists (grade 1 peripheral neuropathy), though she reports this has been slowly improving compared to the immediate post-treatment period.
- Endocrine: TSH was mildly elevated at 5.8 mIU/L at her last visit; referral to endocrinology was placed. She has not yet been seen in endocrinology but reports she has been informed that her thyroid function will be followed closely.

She denies new neck swelling or lumps, new throat or mouth lesions, new ear symptoms, dysphagia to liquids, aspiration, hemoptysis, new oral bleeding, nasal congestion, epistaxis, diplopia, facial numbness, shoulder weakness, or any new symptoms that might suggest nodal recurrence or second primary. She denies chest pain, dyspnea, or bone pain.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive: mild xerostomia (chronic, stable), grade 1 peripheral neuropathy in fingertips (improving), mild high-frequency hearing impairment (not bothersome functionally), occasional difficulty with very dry foods.
Negative: no throat pain, no neck mass, no dysphagia to liquids or standard-consistency solids, no voice changes, no otalgia, no hemoptysis, no unintentional weight loss, no fever or chills, no night sweats, no new lymphadenopathy, no neurologic deficits.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Alert, well-appearing woman in no distress. Affects pleasant and engaged. Nutritional status appears improved from prior visits.
Vital signs: Weight 58 kg (stable from last visit; pre-treatment baseline was 62 kg, representing a net 4 kg loss from baseline, improved from nadir of 55 kg immediately post-treatment). BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, SpO2 98% on room air.

Head and Neck:
- Oral cavity: Oral mucosa moist with only mild erythema of the posterior oropharynx consistent with chronic post-radiation change. No frank ulceration, no visible tumor, no suspicious mucosal lesion. Dentition intact; patient compliance with fluoride protocol confirmed.
- Salivary glands: Parotid and submandibular glands are non-tender, no nodularity or enlargement bilaterally. Reduced but present salivary pooling consistent with grade 1 xerostomia.
- Neck: Supple. No palpable cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy bilaterally. No masses. Moderate soft tissue fibrosis palpable in the bilateral anterior and lateral neck, consistent with post-radiation change — this is similar in character to prior assessments and not worsening. No tenderness on palpation. No carotid bruit detected (note: patient counseled previously on long-term monitoring of carotid vasculature in field).
- Skin: Grade 1 hyperpigmentation of the bilateral anterior neck in the prior radiation field. No moist desquamation. No skin breakdown.
- Eyes, ears: Pupils equal and reactive. No trismus (mouth opening estimated at approximately 3.5 cm, normal, no interval change). External ear canals clear bilaterally.

Flexible Nasopharyngoscopy (performed in clinic today):
The nasopharynx, posterior choanae, and eustachian tube orifices are symmetric and without lesion. The oropharynx is visualized with attention to the base of tongue bilaterally: the left base of tongue mucosa appears smooth, without exophytic lesion, ulceration, or abnormal vascularity. No asymmetric fullness. The vallecula and epiglottis are normal. The hypopharynx and larynx are normal; true vocal cord mobility is symmetric and complete bilaterally. No pooling of secretions. No evidence of recurrent or residual disease identified endoscopically.

Extremities: No clubbing or edema. Mild decreased sensation to light touch at the fingertips bilaterally, consistent with residual grade 1 cisplatin-related peripheral neuropathy.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII intact. No focal deficits. Normal gait.
Lymph nodes: Bilateral axillary, inguinal nodes not palpable.
Cardiovascular/respiratory: Heart sounds regular without murmur. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally.

---

RELEVANT LABORATORY DATA

TSH (today): 5.8 mIU/L — mildly elevated, consistent with evolving radiation-induced hypothyroidism. Free T4 pending at time of note.
CBC, BMP: not formally rechecked today; last values at 4-week post-treatment visit were within acceptable limits (Cr 0.85 mg/dL). Will recheck at 12-month visit.
Audiogram: on file from post-treatment assessment — mild bilateral high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss (4 kHz and 8 kHz), grade 1 per CTCAE; not clinically progressive per patient report.

---

IMAGING

Most recent imaging: CT neck with contrast obtained at 6-month post-treatment surveillance — showed no evidence of recurrent primary oropharyngeal malignancy, no pathologic lymphadenopathy (left level IIA node now 0.7 cm), stable post-radiation fibrotic changes. Prior PET-CT at 3 months post-treatment demonstrated complete metabolic response.

No new cross-sectional imaging ordered today. Per surveillance plan, annual PET-CT or CT neck with contrast is planned at 12 months post-treatment (approximately 3 months from today).

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HPV-positive squamous cell carcinoma, left base of tongue, Stage III (T2N1M0), s/p definitive chemoradiation (IMRT 70 Gy + cisplatin x2 cycles), complete metabolic response by PET-CT at 3 months, confirmed no recurrence on CT at 6 months.
   - Clinically and endoscopically no evidence of recurrent disease today at 9 months.
   - Ongoing surveillance per established plan. Next imaging: PET-CT or CT neck with contrast at the 12-month post-treatment mark (approximately 3 months from today).
   - Will continue clinical exams every 3 months in year 2 as planned.
   - Patient remains at low but not negligible risk for late recurrence; she understands the importance of sustained surveillance and prompt reporting of new symptoms.

2. Radiation-induced hypothyroidism (evolving).
   - TSH now 5.8 mIU/L, mildly elevated. Free T4 pending.
   - Endocrinology referral placed at last visit; patient has not yet been seen. Will re-refer and ensure appointment is scheduled prior to next visit.
   - Given TSH in the mildly elevated range and symptoms of fatigue still present (though attributed partly to recovery), will initiate a discussion of low-dose levothyroxine with the endocrinology team; may consider starting empirically if patient remains symptomatic and TSH does not normalize spontaneously. Will await free T4 result this week.
   - Patient educated on symptoms of overt hypothyroidism to monitor (fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, weight gain, mood changes).

3. Grade 1 xerostomia — chronic, stable.
   - Continue Biotene oral spray, frequent water intake. Fluoride trays ongoing per dentistry.
   - Dental surveillance maintained at least semi-annually given radiation to oral cavity field.
   - Patient counseled on long-term dental hygiene and risk of osteoradionecrosis (low-level ongoing risk); instructed to notify us prior to any planned tooth extraction.

4. Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy — cisplatin-related, slowly improving.
   - No intervention required. Will continue to monitor.
   - Patient denies functional impairment.

5. Mild high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss — cisplatin/radiation-related, grade 1, not functionally limiting.
   - No intervention required at this time. Will repeat audiogram at annual visit.

6. Post-radiation cervical fibrosis.
   - Patient performing neck stretching exercises as instructed by physical therapy. No worsening trismus. Range of neck motion intact.

7. Performance status and quality of life.
   - ECOG PS 1. Patient has returned to part-time teaching; doing well emotionally and professionally. Cancer survivorship support resources reviewed. Patient is actively engaged in HPV vaccination advocacy in her school community, which is acknowledged and encouraged.

Return to clinic in approximately 3 months for 12-month comprehensive annual surveillance visit, including repeat flexible nasopharyngoscopy, laboratory workup (CBC, CMP, TSH, free T4), and cross-sectional imaging (PET-CT planned).

Time spent in face-to-face encounter with patient: approximately 35 minutes, greater than 50% of which was devoted to counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed by: Attending Physician, Head and Neck Medical Oncology
